explanation_box.text: This reptile looks hardy and healthy. And, his color helps him to survive in green foliage surroundings.
This reptile is a contrasting color to his background, which might make him FOOD for predators.
This reptile has a wonky eye. If he produces others with wonky eye, these bad genes might make eyesight poor. This might interfere with seeing mates or avoiding predators.
This reptile looks big. He might be able to fend off predators AND grab a female for procreation.
This reptile looks pretty average in size, color, and pattern. Thus, his fertility is average.
This reptile is relatively large but also unusual in color. The other reptiles this size and color can easily find each other to successfully procreate.
This reptile is easy to spot (in color) by predators. It has a low score because fewer survive.
This reptile has only three toes. It does not walk or run well. It does not get away from predators easily.
This reptile is above average in fertility because its sperm county is unusually high.

This reptile has an outgoing personality which means he runs around mating with many, many other reptiles.

This reptile has a very short tail. This means he is not able to defend himself well or hold onto a mate. Thus, his fertility score is somewhat low.
This reptile metabolizes the greenery behind him as food very well. Thus, he and his offspring are very healthy.
This reptile has survived very well in the weather trends of his location. Thus, he has an above average fertility count.
This reptile is a quinteseential green tree frog. This is the typical fertility score for this animal.

prompt_continuous: Reptiles varied in whether they were GREEN or RED. Did being GREEN or RED make a difference to fertility? If you did not notice this feature or believe it did not make a difference to fertility, please hit no and proceed.
question_text: Did color matter to the fertility of reptiles?

question_text: Did foot shape matter to the fertility of reptiles?
